The **2005–06 season** was the 126th season of competitive [association football in England](/wiki/Association_football_in_England "Association football in England").

## Overview

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2005%E2%80%9306_in_English_football&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Overview")]

* Chelsea land their second Premier League title in as many seasons. They headed the table virtually all season long and amassed 91 points in retaining the trophy.
* The rebuilt [Wembley Stadium](/wiki/Wembley_Stadium "Wembley Stadium") was due to open in time for the [FA Cup](/wiki/FA_Cup "FA Cup") final in May. However, in August 2005, [The Football Association](/wiki/The_Football_Association "The Football Association") reserved the [Millennium Stadium](/wiki/Millennium_Stadium "Millennium Stadium") as a backup, as there was some doubt whether Wembley would be ready. The doubts were confirmed on 21 February 2006, when The FA announced that the final would indeed be held at Millennium Stadium. On 31 March 2006 The FA confirmed that the new Wembley would not be opened until 2007.
* Two clubs opened new stadiums at the beginning of this season:
  + [Coventry City](/wiki/Coventry_City_F.C. "Coventry City F.C.") – [Ricoh Arena](/wiki/Ricoh_Arena "Ricoh Arena").
  + [Swansea City](/wiki/Swansea_City_A.F.C. "Swansea City A.F.C.") – [Liberty Stadium](/wiki/Liberty_Stadium "Liberty Stadium").
* [F.C. United of Manchester](/wiki/F.C._United_of_Manchester "F.C. United of Manchester"), formed by disgruntled [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United_F.C. "Manchester United F.C.") fans, played their first competitive season, competing in the [North West Counties Football League](/wiki/North_West_Counties_Football_League "North West Counties Football League") Division Two (level 10 of the [English football league system](/wiki/English_football_league_system "English football league system")), from which they gained promotion at the first time of asking.[[1]](#cite_note-BBC-1)
* [Reading](/wiki/Reading_F.C. "Reading F.C.") broke the previous points record of 105 points, taking 106 points from 46 games, scoring 99 goals to earn [promotion](/wiki/Promotion_(sports) "Promotion (sports)") to the [Premier League](/wiki/Premier_League "Premier League").
* [Wigan Athletic](/wiki/Wigan_Athletic_F.C. "Wigan Athletic F.C."), who earned [promotion](/wiki/Promotion_(sports) "Promotion (sports)") to the Premier League by finishing second in the [Football League Championship](/wiki/Football_League_Championship "Football League Championship"), played their first ever season in the top division of English football and comfortably stayed up, staying clear from the threat of relegation all season.
  + Wigan were the first club to play an inaugural season in the top tier of English football since [Barnsley](/wiki/Barnsley_F.C._seasons "Barnsley F.C. seasons") in [1997–98](/wiki/1997%E2%80%9398_FA_Premier_League "1997–98 FA Premier League"), and the first to avoid relegation in an inaugural top tier season since [Millwall](/wiki/Millwall_F.C._seasons "Millwall F.C. seasons") in [1988–89](/wiki/1988%E2%80%9389_Football_League "1988–89 Football League").

## Diary of the season

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2005%E2%80%9306_in_English_football&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Diary of the season")]

* 29 June 2005 — [Chelsea](/wiki/Chelsea_F.C. "Chelsea F.C.") sell [Mateja Kezman](/wiki/Mateja_Kezman "Mateja Kezman") to [Atlético Madrid](/wiki/Atl%C3%A9tico_Madrid "Atlético Madrid") for £5.3 million.
* 1 July 2005 — [Tottenham Hotspur](/wiki/Tottenham_Hotspur_F.C. "Tottenham Hotspur F.C.") sign 18-year-old [Leeds United](/wiki/Leeds_United_A.F.C. "Leeds United A.F.C.") winger [Aaron Lennon](/wiki/Aaron_Lennon "Aaron Lennon") for £1 million.
* 4 July 2005 — [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool_F.C. "Liverpool F.C.") sign goalkeeper [Pepe Reina](/wiki/Pepe_Reina "Pepe Reina") from [Villarreal](/wiki/Villarreal_CF "Villarreal CF") for £6 million and [Mark González](/wiki/Mark_Gonz%C3%A1lez "Mark González") from [Albacete](/wiki/Albacete_Balompi%C3%A9 "Albacete Balompié") for £4.5 million.
* 5 July 2005 — [Park Ji-Sung](/wiki/Park_Ji-Sung "Park Ji-Sung") becomes [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United_F.C. "Manchester United F.C.")'s first Asian player in a £4 million move from [PSV Eindhoven](/wiki/PSV_Eindhoven "PSV Eindhoven").
* 8 July 2005 — [Blackburn Rovers](/wiki/Blackburn_Rovers_F.C. "Blackburn Rovers F.C.") sign [Craig Bellamy](/wiki/Craig_Bellamy "Craig Bellamy") from [Newcastle United](/wiki/Newcastle_United_F.C. "Newcastle United F.C.") for £5 million.
* 12 July 2005 — [Arsenal](/wiki/Arsenal_F.C. "Arsenal F.C.") sign [Stuttgart](/wiki/VfB_Stuttgart "VfB Stuttgart") and [Belarus](/wiki/Belarus_national_football_team "Belarus national football team") midfielder [Alexander Hleb](/wiki/Alexander_Hleb "Alexander Hleb") for £11.2 million.
* 15 July 2005 — [Patrick Vieira](/wiki/Patrick_Vieira "Patrick Vieira") ends nine years at Arsenal in a £13.7 million move to [Juventus](/wiki/Juventus_FC "Juventus FC").
* 18 July 2005 — Chelsea sign [Shaun Wright-Phillips](/wiki/Shaun_Wright-Phillips "Shaun Wright-Phillips") from [Manchester City](/wiki/Manchester_City_F.C. "Manchester City F.C.") for £21 million.
* 20 July 2005 — Liverpool sign [Peter Crouch](/wiki/Peter_Crouch "Peter Crouch") from [Southampton](/wiki/Southampton_F.C. "Southampton F.C.") for £7 million.
* 27 July 2005 — Manchester City sign [Darius Vassell](/wiki/Darius_Vassell "Darius Vassell") from [Aston Villa](/wiki/Aston_Villa_F.C. "Aston Villa F.C.") for £2 million.
* 4 August 2005 — [Phil Neville](/wiki/Phil_Neville "Phil Neville"), who has spent his entire 12-year career at Manchester United, is sold to [Everton](/wiki/Everton_F.C. "Everton F.C.") for £3.5 million.
* 7 August 2005 — Chelsea claim the first silverware of the season when they beat Arsenal 2–1 at the [Millennium Stadium](/wiki/Millennium_Stadium "Millennium Stadium") in Cardiff to win the [FA Community Shield](/wiki/FA_Community_Shield "FA Community Shield").
* 8 August 2005 — Manchester United misfit [Kléberson](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Kl%C3%A9berson "José Kléberson") is sold to [Beşiktaş](/wiki/Be%C5%9Fikta%C5%9F_J.K. "Beşiktaş J.K.") of [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey") for £2.5 million.
* 17 August 2005 — [Liberty Stadium](/wiki/Liberty_Stadium "Liberty Stadium") hosts its first game; a friendly between [Wales](/wiki/Wales_national_football_team "Wales national football team") and [Slovenia](/wiki/Slovenia_national_football_team "Slovenia national football team").[[2]](#cite_note-2) It is the new stadium of [Swansea City](/wiki/Swansea_City_A.F.C. "Swansea City A.F.C.") (replacing 93-year-old [Vetch Field](/wiki/Vetch_Field "Vetch Field")), as well as the [Ospreys](/wiki/Ospreys_(rugby_union) "Ospreys (rugby union)") rugby union team.[[3]](#cite_note-3)
* 18 August 2005 — Tottenham Hotspur sell [Frédéric Kanouté](/wiki/Fr%C3%A9d%C3%A9ric_Kanout%C3%A9 "Frédéric Kanouté") to [Sevilla](/wiki/Sevilla_FC "Sevilla FC") for £4.4 million.
* 19 August 2005 — Chelsea pay a club record £24.4 million to [Lyon](/wiki/Olympique_Lyonnais "Olympique Lyonnais") for [Ghanaian](/wiki/Ghana "Ghana") midfielder [Michael Essien](/wiki/Michael_Essien "Michael Essien").
* 20 August 2005 — [Coventry City](/wiki/Coventry_City_F.C. "Coventry City F.C.") take on [Queens Park Rangers](/wiki/Queens_Park_Rangers_F.C. "Queens Park Rangers F.C.") in their first game at the new 32,500-seat [Ricoh Arena](/wiki/Ricoh_Arena "Ricoh Arena").[[4]](#cite_note-4)
* 23 August 2005 — [Tiago Mendes](/wiki/Tiago_Mendes "Tiago Mendes") leaves Chelsea for Lyon in a £6.5 million deal, while [Milan Baroš](/wiki/Milan_Baro%C5%A1 "Milan Baroš") moves from Liverpool to Aston Villa for the same fee.
* 26 August 2005 — Liverpool win the [UEFA Super Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Super_Cup "UEFA Super Cup") beating [CSKA Moscow](/wiki/PFC_CSKA_Moscow "PFC CSKA Moscow") 3–1.
* 31 August 2005 — August draws to a close with Chelsea looking well placed to defend their [Premier League](/wiki/Premier_League "Premier League") title having won their first four games of the season. [Stuart Pearce](/wiki/Stuart_Pearce "Stuart Pearce")'s Manchester City have made an impressive start to the season as they occupy second place. [Charlton Athletic](/wiki/Charlton_Athletic_F.C. "Charlton Athletic F.C."), Manchester United and [Bolton Wanderers](/wiki/Bolton_Wanderers_F.C. "Bolton Wanderers F.C.") complete the top five. Newly promoted [Sunderland](/wiki/Sunderland_A.F.C. "Sunderland A.F.C.") are bottom after losing their first four games of the league season, with Newcastle United and [Portsmouth](/wiki/Portsmouth_F.C. "Portsmouth F.C.") completing the bottom three. Newcastle United break their club transfer record by paying £17 million to [Real Madrid](/wiki/Real_Madrid_C.F. "Real Madrid C.F.") for [Michael Owen](/wiki/Michael_Owen "Michael Owen").[[5]](#cite_note-5) In the Championship, Sheffield United lead the table with Reading in second place, ahead of newly promoted Luton Town on goal difference. Watford, Southampton and Wolves complete the playoff places. Norwich City, narrowly relegated from the Premier League the previous season, have made a poor start to a campaign thought by many to be a challenge for an immediate return to England's elite and stand one place off the bottom of the Championship, sandwiched by Millwall (bottom) and Sheffield Wednesday.
* 7 September 2005 — [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_national_football_team "Northern Ireland national football team") take a historic 1–0 win over [England](/wiki/England_national_football_team "England national football team") at [Windsor Park](/wiki/Windsor_Park "Windsor Park"), [Belfast](/wiki/Belfast "Belfast"), strengthening calls for [Sven-Göran Eriksson](/wiki/Sven-G%C3%B6ran_Eriksson "Sven-Göran Eriksson")'s resignation.
* 30 September 2005 — Chelsea's 100% start to the season continues after seven games as they finish September with their Premier League leadership intact. Charlton Athletic are their nearest challengers in second place, with Bolton Wanderers, [West Ham United](/wiki/West_Ham_United_F.C. "West Ham United F.C.") (newly promoted) and Manchester United completing the top five. Sunderland remain bottom with just one point so far this season, while Everton and [West Bromwich Albion](/wiki/West_Bromwich_Albion_F.C. "West Bromwich Albion F.C.") complete the bottom three.[[6]](#cite_note-6) In the Championship, Sheffield United hold a six-point advantage over Reading. Luton, Wolves, Watford and Leeds make up the rest of the top six. Sheffield Wednesday, Plymouth Argyle and Crewe Alexandra prop up the table.
* 8 October 2005 — England defeat [Austria](/wiki/Austria_national_football_team "Austria national football team") 1–0 at [Old Trafford](/wiki/Old_Trafford "Old Trafford"), with the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands_national_football_team "Netherlands national football team") defeating the [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic_national_football_team "Czech Republic national football team") the same night, thus assuring England automatic qualification for the [2006 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2006_FIFA_World_Cup "2006 FIFA World Cup"), either as the top team in their qualifying group or one of the top two second-placed European teams.
* 12 October 2005 — England defeat [Poland](/wiki/Poland_national_football_team "Poland national football team") 2–1 at Old Trafford to finish top of their World Cup qualifying group.
* 17 October 2005 — [Middlesbrough](/wiki/Middlesbrough_F.C. "Middlesbrough F.C.") defender [Abel Xavier](/wiki/Abel_Xavier "Abel Xavier") is banned from all football after failing a drugs test.
* 27 October 2005 — England legend [Paul Gascoigne](/wiki/Paul_Gascoigne "Paul Gascoigne"), 38, is named manager of [Conference North](/wiki/Conference_North "Conference North") club [Kettering Town](/wiki/Kettering_Town "Kettering Town").[[7]](#cite_note-7)
* 28 October 2005 — [Deputy Prime Minister](/wiki/Deputy_Prime_Minister_of_the_United_Kingdom "Deputy Prime Minister of the United Kingdom") [John Prescott](/wiki/John_Prescott "John Prescott") gives final approval for [Brighton & Hove Albion](/wiki/Brighton_%26_Hove_Albion_F.C. "Brighton & Hove Albion F.C.") to build [Falmer Stadium](/wiki/Falmer_Stadium "Falmer Stadium"), now scheduled to open in 2008.
* 29 October 2005 — Manchester United become the first team to score 1,000 Premier League goals, in its 14th season.
* 31 October 2005 — October draws to a close with Chelsea now runaway leaders with 10 wins and a draw from their first 11 games, while their nearest challengers are [Wigan Athletic](/wiki/Wigan_Athletic_F.C. "Wigan Athletic F.C."), playing their first top-division season and only their 28th as a professional league club. Tottenham Hotspur, Bolton Wanderers and Charlton Athletic complete the top five, while a disappointing Manchester United and Arsenal are sixth and seventh respectively. Sunderland have at last recorded their first league win of the season but still occupy bottom place, with [Birmingham City](/wiki/Birmingham_City_F.C. "Birmingham City F.C.") and Everton completing the bottom three.[[8]](#cite_note-8) In the Championship, Reading have closed the gap between them and Sheffield United to three points, with Luton, Watford, Leeds and Crystal Palace completing the top six and Brighton, Crewe and Millwall in the relegation zone.
* 6 November 2005 — Manchester United become the first team to beat Chelsea in 41 Premier League games, with a [Darren Fletcher](/wiki/Darren_Fletcher "Darren Fletcher") header securing a 1–0 victory at Old Trafford.
* 18 November 2005 — [Roy Keane](/wiki/Roy_Keane "Roy Keane") leaves Manchester United by mutual consent, ending his 12-year association with the club.
* 24 November 2005 — [Alain Perrin](/wiki/Alain_Perrin "Alain Perrin") is sacked as manager of Portsmouth after eight months in charge.
* 30 November 2005 — November ends with Chelsea still top of the Premier League, their nearest contenders now being Manchester United who are 10 points behind and with a game in hand. Arsenal, Wigan Athletic and Tottenham Hotspur complete the top five. Sunderland remain bottom with a mere five points and one win so far, with Birmingham City and Portsmouth completing the bottom three.[[9]](#cite_note-9) In the Championship, Reading have overtaken Sheffield United with a four-point advantage. Watford, Leeds, Luton and Cardiff complete the top six, while Crewe, Brighton and Millwall remain in the relegation zone.
* 3 December 2005 — [Harry Redknapp](/wiki/Harry_Redknapp "Harry Redknapp") walks out of Southampton after being refused permission to discuss terms with Portsmouth over returning as manager.
* 5 December 2005 — Paul Gascoigne resigns after just over a month in charge of Kettering Town, during which time they won two, lost two and drew two of their six games.[[10]](#cite_note-10)
* 7 December 2005 — Harry Redknapp is appointed manager of Portsmouth for the second time under contract until the end of the season.
* 22 December 2005 — [George Burley](/wiki/George_Burley "George Burley") is appointed Harry Redknapp's replacement at Southampton.
* 31 December 2005 — Chelsea's Premier League continues into 2006 by 11 points over Manchester United, while Liverpool's good run of form has taken them into third place. Tottenham Hotspur and Wigan Athletic complete the top five. Sunderland, Birmingham City and Portsmouth occupy the relegation places.[[11]](#cite_note-11) Reading still lead the Championship, with Sheffield United, Leeds, Watford, Crystal Palace and Wolves completing the top six. Sheffield Wednesday, Millwall and Crewe finish the year in the relegation zone.
* 4 January 2006 — [Alexandre Gaydamak](/wiki/Alexandre_Gaydamak "Alexandre Gaydamak") takes a 50% stake in Portsmouth alongside [Milan Mandarić](/wiki/Milan_Mandari%C4%87 "Milan Mandarić"). Manchester United pay [Spartak Moscow](/wiki/FC_Spartak_Moscow "FC Spartak Moscow") £7 million for [Serbian](/wiki/Serbia "Serbia") defender [Nemanja Vidić](/wiki/Nemanja_Vidi%C4%87 "Nemanja Vidić").
* 6 January 2006 — Portsmouth sign [Benjani](/wiki/Benjani_Mwaruwari "Benjani Mwaruwari") from [Auxerre](/wiki/AJ_Auxerre "AJ Auxerre") for £4.1 million.
* 8 January 2006 — [Burton Albion](/wiki/Burton_Albion_F.C. "Burton Albion F.C.") of the [Conference National](/wiki/Conference_National "Conference National") hold Manchester United to a 0–0 draw in the FA Cup third round, earning a lucrative replay at Old Trafford on 18 January 2006 which they lose 5–0.
* 10 January 2006 — Manchester United sign French defender [Patrice Evra](/wiki/Patrice_Evra "Patrice Evra") from [AS Monaco](/wiki/AS_Monaco_FC "AS Monaco FC") for £5.5 million.
* 12 January 2006 — Liverpool sign [Daniel Agger](/wiki/Daniel_Agger "Daniel Agger") from [Brøndby](/wiki/Br%C3%B8ndby_IF "Brøndby IF") for £5.8 million.
* 13 January 2006 — Arsenal sign [Emmanuel Adebayor](/wiki/Emmanuel_Adebayor "Emmanuel Adebayor") from AS Monaco for £7 million.
* 20 January 2006 — The Premier League formally charges Portsmouth with "tapping up" Harry Redknapp. Arsenal pay £5 million to Southampton for striker [Theo Walcott](/wiki/Theo_Walcott "Theo Walcott"), who turns 17 on 16 March.
* 22 January 2006 — West Ham United pay £7.25 million for [Norwich City](/wiki/Norwich_City_F.C. "Norwich City F.C.") striker [Dean Ashton](/wiki/Dean_Ashton "Dean Ashton").
* 23 January 2006 — The FA announces that Sven-Göran Eriksson will leave his post as England national coach after the 2006 FIFA World Cup.
* 25 January 2006 — [Craig Levein](/wiki/Craig_Levein "Craig Levein") is sacked as manager of [Leicester City](/wiki/Leicester_City_F.C. "Leicester City F.C.").
* 30 January 2006 — [Phil Brown](/wiki/Phil_Brown_(footballer_born_1959) "Phil Brown (footballer born 1959)") is sacked as manager of [Derby County](/wiki/Derby_County_F.C. "Derby County F.C."). Academy boss [Terry Westley](/wiki/Terry_Westley "Terry Westley") is appointed interim manager, with player [Paul Peschisolido](/wiki/Paul_Peschisolido "Paul Peschisolido") acting as his assistant. Manchester City sign [Greek](/wiki/Greece "Greece") midfielder [Georgios Samaras](/wiki/Georgios_Samaras "Georgios Samaras") from [Heerenveen](/wiki/SC_Heerenveen "SC Heerenveen") for £6 million.
* 31 January 2006 — Chelsea now lead the Premier League by 14 points ahead of Manchester United and 18 points ahead of Liverpool, with Tottenham Hotspur and Arsenal completing the top five. Sunderland, Portsmouth and Birmingham City occupy the three relegation places.[[12]](#cite_note-12) Reading lead the Championship by ten points and look almost certain to gain promotion to the top flight for the first time in their history, while Sheffield United (with a game in hand) are eleven points clear of Leeds and Watford in joint third place. Preston North End and Cardiff City complete the top six. Brighton, Millwall and Crewe prop up the table.
* 2 February 2006 — [Graeme Souness](/wiki/Graeme_Souness "Graeme Souness") is sacked as manager of Newcastle United, and [Glenn Roeder](/wiki/Glenn_Roeder "Glenn Roeder") was made caretaker manager with [Alan Shearer](/wiki/Alan_Shearer "Alan Shearer") his assistant manager.
* 4 February 2006 — Alan Shearer becomes Newcastle United's top goalscorer of all time, scoring his 201st goal for the club, against Portsmouth at St. James' Park, beating [Jackie Milburn](/wiki/Jackie_Milburn "Jackie Milburn")'s 200 goal tally which had stood for 49 years.
* 11 February 2006 — Middlesbrough comfortably defeat reigning Premier League champions Chelsea 3–0 at the [Riverside Stadium](/wiki/Riverside_Stadium "Riverside Stadium"), the first time [José Mourinho](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Mourinho "José Mourinho")'s Chelsea have lost by more than a single goal.
* 18 February 2006 — Liverpool beat Manchester United in the FA Cup for the first time since 1921. The draw was a 5th round matchup.
* 21 February 2006 — The FA announce that the [2006 FA Cup Final](/wiki/2006_FA_Cup_Final "2006 FA Cup Final"), set for 13 May, will be held at the Millennium Stadium, as the builders of the new [Wembley Stadium](/wiki/Wembley_Stadium "Wembley Stadium") are unable to guarantee that it will be ready for the match. They also announce that the national team's pre-World Cup friendlies originally scheduled for Wembley will instead be held at Old Trafford.
* 21 February 2006 — A [Football League](/wiki/The_Football_League "The Football League") under-21 team, composed of players from the Football League's three divisions, beats a [Lega Nazionale Professionisti](/wiki/Lega_Nazionale_Professionisti "Lega Nazionale Professionisti") under-21 team, composed of players from [Serie B](/wiki/Serie_B "Serie B"), 1–0 at the [KC Stadium](/wiki/KC_Stadium "KC Stadium").
* 26 February 2006 — Manchester United defeat Wigan Athletic 4–0 at the Millennium Stadium to win the [Carling Cup](/wiki/Football_League_Cup "Football League Cup"). Should they finish in the top five this season, there will be a [UEFA Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Europa_League "UEFA Europa League") place for the league's sixth highest placed team.
* 28 February 2006 — The month ends with Chelsea's lead over Manchester United at the top of the Premier League narrowed slightly to 12 points. Liverpool, Tottenham Hotspur and Arsenal once again complete the top five, while Bolton Wanderers occupy the now sought-after sixth place. Sunderland continue to prop up the top flight, having gained just nine points so far this season. Portsmouth and Birmingham City complete the bottom three, with Portsmouth now eight points adrift of safety.[[13]](#cite_note-13) The top six remain unchanged from the previous month, although Watford have moved to third and Crystal Palace to fifth. Millwall has changed places in the relegation zone with Brighton, whom they lead on goal difference, while Crewe prop up the table.
* 6 March 2006 — [Mick McCarthy](/wiki/Mick_McCarthy "Mick McCarthy") is sacked as manager of Sunderland after his team accumulated just 10 points in 28 matches, with [Kevin Ball](/wiki/Kevin_Ball "Kevin Ball") made caretaker manager for the remainder of the season.
* 25 March 2006 — [Reading](/wiki/Reading_F.C. "Reading F.C.") became the first side to be [promoted](/wiki/Promotion_and_relegation "Promotion and relegation") to the 2006–07 Premier League after drawing 1–1 at Leicester City. 2006–07 will be the [Berkshire](/wiki/Berkshire "Berkshire") club's first ever season in England's top flight (though they would have been automatically promoted in 1995 as Division One runners-up had it not been for a reorganisation of the league which saw them forced into the playoffs where they had lost to Bolton Wanderers in the final).
* 31 March 2006 — March draws to a close with Chelsea now nine points ahead of Manchester United at the top of the Premier League. Liverpool, Tottenham, Blackburn Rovers and Arsenal complete the top six, while Wigan Athletic are now eighth and their dreams of European football are fading fast. Sunderland are still bottom with 10 points though their relegation has yet to be confirmed. Portsmouth and Birmingham City complete the bottom three, both of them being three points behind 17th-placed West Bromwich Albion and with a game in hand.[[14]](#cite_note-14) Reading need only one more win from their last six games to confirm the Championship title, while Sheffield United appear almost certain to join Reading in the top flight. Watford, Leeds, Crystal Palace and Preston North End complete the top six. At the other end of the table, Crewe and Brighton look dead and buried, needing four wins each from their last six games and other results to go their way to avoid relegation. Millwall are only five points adrift in the relegation zone but still look bound for relegation as well, just two seasons after competing in the [UEFA Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Cup "UEFA Cup").
* 1 April 2006 — Reading win the [Football League Championship](/wiki/Football_League_Championship "Football League Championship") title. A 5-0 victory over Derby County with [Sheffield United](/wiki/Sheffield_United_F.C. "Sheffield United F.C.") only drawing mean that Reading cannot be overtaken.
* 14 April 2006 — Sunderland are [relegated](/wiki/Promotion_and_relegation "Promotion and relegation") from the Premier League after a 0–0 draw with Manchester United at Old Trafford. This is the first relegation in the league.
* 15 April 2006 — Sheffield United become the second side to secure promotion to the Premier League after they beat [Cardiff City](/wiki/Cardiff_City_F.C. "Cardiff City F.C.") while [Watford](/wiki/Watford_F.C. "Watford F.C.") and Leeds United only draw with [Wolverhampton Wanderers](/wiki/Wolverhampton_Wanderers_F.C. "Wolverhampton Wanderers F.C.") and champions Reading respectively.
* 15 April 2006 — [Accrington Stanley](/wiki/Accrington_Stanley_F.C. "Accrington Stanley F.C.") regain their Football League status 44 years after the previous club of the same name lost it due to bankruptcy, after they beat [Woking](/wiki/Woking_F.C. "Woking F.C.") to win the Conference National title.
* 17 April 2006 — [Sheffield Wednesday](/wiki/Sheffield_Wednesday_F.C. "Sheffield Wednesday F.C.")'s 2–0 win at Brighton & Hove Albion ensures that the trio of Brighton, [Crewe Alexandra](/wiki/Crewe_Alexandra_F.C. "Crewe Alexandra F.C.") and [Millwall](/wiki/Millwall_F.C. "Millwall F.C.") are all relegated from the Championship.
* 17 April 2006 — Alan Shearer plays his last competitive match against, rather fittingly, Sunderland, after a [Julio Arca](/wiki/Julio_Arca "Julio Arca") tackle tore his [medial collateral ligament](/wiki/Medial_collateral_ligament "Medial collateral ligament").
* 18 April 2006 — [John Lyall](/wiki/John_Lyall "John Lyall"), former [Ipswich Town](/wiki/Ipswich_Town_F.C. "Ipswich Town F.C.") and West Ham United manager, dies of a heart attack aged 66.
* 20 April 2006 — [Altrincham](/wiki/Altrincham_F.C. "Altrincham F.C.") of the Conference National have 18 points deducted for fielding an ineligible player 13 times. This points deduction relegates the club to the Conference North.
* 22 April 2006 — [Carlisle United](/wiki/Carlisle_United_F.C. "Carlisle United F.C.") secure promotion to [League One](/wiki/Football_League_One "Football League One") after a 1–1 draw at [Mansfield Town](/wiki/Mansfield_Town_F.C. "Mansfield Town F.C.") while [Leyton Orient](/wiki/Leyton_Orient_F.C. "Leyton Orient F.C.") were also held to a draw by the same scoreline at [Lincoln City](/wiki/Lincoln_City_F.C. "Lincoln City F.C."). This promotion comes just one season after Carlisle returned to the Football League following a one-season exile.
* 22 April 2006 — [Walsall](/wiki/Walsall_F.C. "Walsall F.C.") are relegated to [League Two](/wiki/Football_League_Two "Football League Two") after losing 3–1 at home to [Huddersfield Town](/wiki/Huddersfield_Town_F.C. "Huddersfield Town F.C.") and [Rotherham United](/wiki/Rotherham_United_F.C. "Rotherham United F.C.") draw with [Scunthorpe United](/wiki/Scunthorpe_United_F.C. "Scunthorpe United F.C.").
* 29 April 2006 — Chelsea successfully retain their Premier League title after beating Manchester United 3–0. On the same occasion, Portsmouth win 2 - 1 at Wigan to confirm their safety and relegated both West Brom and Birmingham, who can only hold on to a goalless draw against Newcastle.
* 29 April 2006 — Charlton Athletic manager [Alan Curbishley](/wiki/Alan_Curbishley "Alan Curbishley") announces that he will resign at the end of the season, ending a 15-year-long tenure as manager at [The Valley](/wiki/The_Valley_(London) "The Valley (London)").
* 29 April 2006 — [Southend United](/wiki/Southend_United_F.C. "Southend United F.C.") secure promotion from League One after they draw 2–2 at Swansea City while [Brentford](/wiki/Brentford_F.C. "Brentford F.C.") also draw against [Hartlepool United](/wiki/Hartlepool_United_F.C. "Hartlepool United F.C."). Also in League One, [Swindon Town](/wiki/Swindon_Town_F.C. "Swindon Town F.C.") are relegated to League Two after they could only draw 1–1 against [Bristol City](/wiki/Bristol_City_F.C. "Bristol City F.C.").
* 29 April 2006 — [Northampton Town](/wiki/Northampton_Town_F.C. "Northampton Town F.C.") secure promotion from League Two after beating [Chester City](/wiki/Chester_City_F.C. "Chester City F.C.") 1–0. [Rushden & Diamonds](/wiki/Rushden_%26_Diamonds_F.C. "Rushden & Diamonds F.C.") are relegated to the Conference National when they lose 2–0 away to [Boston United](/wiki/Boston_United_F.C. "Boston United F.C.") and other results go against them.
* 30 April 2006 — Reading break Sunderland's record for most points in any professional English league, beating Queens Park Rangers 2–1 to finish on 106 points.
* 30 April 2006 — April draws to a close with Chelsea confirmed as champions, while Manchester United and Liverpool are the next highest teams level on points. Tottenham Hotspur, Arsenal and Blackburn Rovers complete the top six. Sunderland are bottom and were relegated two weeks ago, while West Bromwich Albion and Birmingham City's relegation has just been confirmed. It is a brilliant time for Portsmouth, who looked dead and buried barely two months ago.[[15]](#cite_note-15)
* 4 May 2006 — Current Middlesbrough manager [Steve McClaren](/wiki/Steve_McClaren "Steve McClaren") signs a four-year contract agreeing to succeed Sven-Göran Eriksson as England head coach after the World Cup. The contract begins on 1 August.
* 4 May 2006 — Newcastle United are refused permission to appoint caretaker manager Glenn Roeder on a permanent basis as he does not possess the required coaching qualifications.[[16]](#cite_note-16)
* 6 May 2006 — Southend United, after defeating Bristol City, become League One champions, while [Colchester United](/wiki/Colchester_United_F.C. "Colchester United F.C.") secure the second automatic place to the Championship after managing a draw at Yeovil while Brentford can only draw away at [AFC Bournemouth](/wiki/AFC_Bournemouth "AFC Bournemouth"); Colchester will play in the top two tiers in English football for the first time in their history after their promotion. Around the same time, both Hartlepool United and [Milton Keynes Dons](/wiki/Milton_Keynes_Dons_F.C. "Milton Keynes Dons F.C.") are relegated to League Two after both teams can only draw while Rotherham United survive.
* 6 May 2006 — [Oxford United](/wiki/Oxford_United_F.C. "Oxford United F.C.") lose their league status after 44 years and are relegated from the football league following a 3–2 defeat by Leyton Orient, who are promoted to League One after their win while Grimsby Town can only draw against already promoted Northampton Town.
* 7 May 2006 — Sunderland finish the season on 15 points, the lowest points total ever in top-flight English football since 3 points for a win was introduced in the 1981–82 season.
* 9 May 2006 — Roy Keane's [testimonial](/wiki/Testimonial_match "Testimonial match") is played at Old Trafford, with Manchester United beating [Celtic](/wiki/Celtic_F.C. "Celtic F.C.") 1–0 thanks to a second half [Cristiano Ronaldo](/wiki/Cristiano_Ronaldo "Cristiano Ronaldo") goal.
* 10 May 2006 — Middlesbrough lose the UEFA Cup final 4–0 to Sevilla in Steve McClaren's last game in charge at the [Philips Stadion](/wiki/Philips_Stadion "Philips Stadion") in [Eindhoven](/wiki/Eindhoven "Eindhoven"). On the same day, the Premier League change their mind over the Glenn Roeder situation at Newcastle United and give him permission to take on the manager's role on a permanent basis despite him not having the requiring coaching qualifications.[[17]](#cite_note-17)
* 11 May 2006 — Alan Shearer's [testimonial](/wiki/Testimonial_match "Testimonial match") is played at [St James' Park](/wiki/St_James%27_Park "St James' Park"), with a Newcastle XI beating Celtic 3–2, with Shearer scoring the winning penalty. [Joe Royle](/wiki/Joe_Royle "Joe Royle") leaves Ipswich Town "by mutual consent".
* 13 May 2006 — Liverpool win the 125th FA Cup final beating West Ham United 3–1 on [penalties](/wiki/Penalty_shootout_(association_football) "Penalty shootout (association football)") after a thrilling 3–3 draw after [extra time](/wiki/Extra_time "Extra time").
* 16 May 2006 — Lincoln City become the first team to lose four consecutive play-off competitions following a 3–1 aggregate defeat to neighbors [Grimsby Town](/wiki/Grimsby_Town_F.C. "Grimsby Town F.C.") in the League Two semi-finals.
* 17 May 2006 — Arsenal lose in the [UEFA Champions League](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League") final to [Barcelona](/wiki/FC_Barcelona "FC Barcelona") 2–1 in the [Stade de France](/wiki/Stade_de_France "Stade de France"). [Jens Lehmann](/wiki/Jens_Lehmann "Jens Lehmann") is sent off controversially after fouling [Samuel Eto'o](/wiki/Samuel_Eto%27o "Samuel Eto'o") and [Ludovic Giuly](/wiki/Ludovic_Giuly "Ludovic Giuly") puts the ball into the back of the net.
* 20 May 2006 — [Hereford United](/wiki/Hereford_United_F.C. "Hereford United F.C.") gain promotion to League Two after beating [Halifax Town](/wiki/Halifax_Town_A.F.C. "Halifax Town A.F.C.") 3–2 in the [Conference Playoff Final](/wiki/2006_Conference_National_play-off_Final "2006 Conference National play-off Final"), after extra time.
* 21 May 2006 — Watford gain promotion to the Premier League after defeating Leeds United 3–0 in the [Championship play-off final](/wiki/2006_Football_League_Championship_play-off_Final "2006 Football League Championship play-off Final").
* 23 May 2006 — Arsenal sign [Tomáš Rosický](/wiki/Tom%C3%A1%C5%A1_Rosick%C3%BD "Tomáš Rosický") from [Borussia Dortmund](/wiki/Borussia_Dortmund "Borussia Dortmund") for £6.8 million.
* 27 May 2006 — [Barnsley](/wiki/Barnsley_F.C. "Barnsley F.C.") win promotion to the Championship after overcoming Swansea City in the League One play-off final. They win 4–3 on penalties after both sides remained level at 2–2 after extra time.
* 28 May 2006 — [Cheltenham Town](/wiki/Cheltenham_Town_F.C. "Cheltenham Town F.C.") win promotion to the League One by defeating Grimsby Town 1–0 in the League Two play-off final.
* 30 May 2006 — Everton pay a club record £8.6 million for [Crystal Palace](/wiki/Crystal_Palace_F.C. "Crystal Palace F.C.") striker [Andrew Johnson](/wiki/Andrew_Johnson_(English_footballer) "Andrew Johnson (English footballer)"), and Chelsea sign [Salomon Kalou](/wiki/Salomon_Kalou "Salomon Kalou") from [Feyenoord](/wiki/Feyenoord "Feyenoord") for £8 million.
* 31 May 2006 — Chelsea pay a national record £30 million for [Milan](/wiki/A.C._Milan "A.C. Milan") and [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine_national_football_team "Ukraine national football team") striker [Andriy Shevchenko](/wiki/Andriy_Shevchenko "Andriy Shevchenko").
* 2 June 2006 — [Billy Davies](/wiki/Billy_Davies "Billy Davies") leaves [Preston North End](/wiki/Preston_North_End_F.C. "Preston North End F.C.") to become manager of Derby County.
* 4 June 2006 — [Scarborough](/wiki/Scarborough_F.C. "Scarborough F.C.") are relegated from the Conference National for a breach of league rules. They take Altrincham's relegation place.
* 8 June 2006 — The [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC")'s *[Match of the Day](/wiki/Match_of_the_Day "Match of the Day")* will show Premier League highlights for at least another four seasons after £171.6 million bid for television rights was accepted.[[18]](#cite_note-18)
* 10 June 2006 — England open their World Cup campaign with a 1–0 win over [Paraguay](/wiki/Paraguay_national_football_team "Paraguay national football team").
* 14 June 2006 — Everton sign defender [Joleon Lescott](/wiki/Joleon_Lescott "Joleon Lescott") from Wolverhampton Wanderers for £5 million, and Chelsea sell striker [Eiður Guðjohnsen](/wiki/Ei%C3%B0ur_Gu%C3%B0johnsen "Eiður Guðjohnsen") to Barcelona for £8 million.
* 15 June 2006 — England beat [Trinidad and Tobago](/wiki/Trinidad_and_Tobago_national_football_team "Trinidad and Tobago national football team") 2–0 to confirm their qualification for the last 16 of the World Cup.
* 20 June 2006 — England draw 2–2 with [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden_men%27s_national_football_team "Sweden men's national football team") in their final group game.
* 22 June 2006 — Liverpool pay £6 million to Blackburn Rovers for winger Craig Bellamy.
* 25 June 2006 — England reach the World Cup quarter-finals for the second tournament in succession with a 1–0 win over [Ecuador](/wiki/Ecuador_national_football_team "Ecuador national football team") in the second round.
* 1 July 2006 — England lose on penalties to [Portugal](/wiki/Portugal_national_football_team "Portugal national football team") after a goalless draw in the World Cup quarter-finals. Tottenham Hotspur sign [Bulgarian](/wiki/Bulgaria "Bulgaria") striker [Dimitar Berbatov](/wiki/Dimitar_Berbatov "Dimitar Berbatov") from [Bayer Leverkusen](/wiki/Bayer_04_Leverkusen "Bayer 04 Leverkusen") for £10.9 million. Former Tottenham Hotspur and England manager [Glenn Hoddle](/wiki/Glenn_Hoddle "Glenn Hoddle") resigns at Wolverhampton Wanderers.

## League tables

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2005%E2%80%9306_in_English_football&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: League tables")]

### FA Premier League

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2005%E2%80%9306_in_English_football&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: FA Premier League")]

Main article: [2005–06 FA Premier League](/wiki/2005%E2%80%9306_FA_Premier_League "2005–06 FA Premier League")

[Chelsea](/wiki/Chelsea_F.C. "Chelsea F.C."), on 91 points, won their second Premier League title in a row. [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United_F.C. "Manchester United F.C."), whose 83-point tally would have been enough for title glory in most seasons, finished runners-up. Liverpool's league form improved drastically following the previous season and they finished in third place, just one point behind United; they also won the [FA Cup](/wiki/FA_Cup "FA Cup"), giving manager [Rafael Benítez](/wiki/Rafael_Ben%C3%ADtez "Rafael Benítez") his second major trophy in just his second season. [Arsenal](/wiki/Arsenal_F.C. "Arsenal F.C.") snatched the last Champions League spot from under the noses of local rivals [Tottenham Hotspur](/wiki/Tottenham_Hotspur_F.C. "Tottenham Hotspur F.C."), after the latter's players went down with a bout of food poisoning. *The Gunners'* 4–2 victory over [Wigan Athletic](/wiki/Wigan_Athletic_F.C. "Wigan Athletic F.C.") on the final day of the season saw them end 92 years of playing at [Highbury](/wiki/Arsenal_Stadium "Arsenal Stadium") before they moved into their new 60,000-seat [Emirates Stadium](/wiki/Emirates_Stadium "Emirates Stadium").

Ultimately joining Tottenham in the [UEFA Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Europa_League "UEFA Europa League") were [Blackburn Rovers](/wiki/Blackburn_Rovers_F.C. "Blackburn Rovers F.C.") and [West Ham United](/wiki/West_Ham_United_F.C. "West Ham United F.C.") who were promoted the previous season and took the European spot by virtue of being FA Cup runners-up. Despite narrowly losing on penalties to Liverpool, the Hammers enjoyed a successful first season back amongst the elite, finishing in a comfortable 9th place.

Fellow newly promoted side [Wigan Athletic](/wiki/Wigan_Athletic_F.C. "Wigan Athletic F.C."), who were many pundits'[*[who?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] tip for relegation in pre-season, defied the odds to finish 10th in their first season in the top-flight. [Everton](/wiki/Everton_F.C. "Everton F.C.") crashed out of the Champions League early and were in the relegation zone by October, but a good run of results both after Halloween and in the new year saw them comfortably finish in 11th. [Middlesbrough](/wiki/Middlesbrough_F.C. "Middlesbrough F.C.") finished 14th after a disappointing league season, but reached the [UEFA Cup Final](/wiki/2006_UEFA_Cup_Final "2006 UEFA Cup Final") – the first European final in their history – where they were beaten 4–0 by [Sevilla](/wiki/Sevilla_FC "Sevilla FC").

In contrast to the other promoted sides, [Sunderland](/wiki/Sunderland_A.F.C. "Sunderland A.F.C."), the previous season's Championship winners, were relegated with a league record low of 15 points, breaking the previous record that they set themselves in [2002–03](/wiki/2002%E2%80%9303_in_English_football#FA_Premier_League "2002–03 in English football"), while [West Bromwich Albion](/wiki/West_Bromwich_Albion_F.C. "West Bromwich Albion F.C.") and [Birmingham City](/wiki/Birmingham_City_F.C. "Birmingham City F.C.") both hit the 30-point mark and battled bravely, but both were unable to avoid relegation after dismal runs of league form (the Baggies going winless from February onwards, with Birmingham unable to shake off poor away form). [Portsmouth](/wiki/Portsmouth_F.C. "Portsmouth F.C.") spent almost the entire season in the relegation zone, but after the return of [Harry Redknapp](/wiki/Harry_Redknapp "Harry Redknapp"), they stayed up after a late run of good results.

### The Football League

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2005%E2%80%9306_in_English_football&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: The Football League")]

#### Football League Championship

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2005%E2%80%9306_in_English_football&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Football League Championship")]

Main article: [2005–06 Football League Championship](/wiki/2005%E2%80%9306_Football_League_Championship "2005–06 Football League Championship")

[Reading](/wiki/Reading_F.C. "Reading F.C.") entered the top flight for the first time in their history, breaking [Sunderland](/wiki/Sunderland_A.F.C. "Sunderland A.F.C.")'s points record of 105 in the process by accumulating 106 points (coincidentally, Sunderland were relegated from the Premier League while breaking the record for *lowest* number of points under the current scoring system). [Sheffield United](/wiki/Sheffield_United_F.C. "Sheffield United F.C.") joined them, returning to the Premier League after twelve years and earning [Neil Warnock](/wiki/Neil_Warnock "Neil Warnock") his fourth promotion as a manager. Surprise package [Watford](/wiki/Watford_F.C. "Watford F.C."), initially tipped for relegation, entered the play-offs and beat Leeds United 3–0 in the [Millennium Stadium](/wiki/Millennium_Stadium "Millennium Stadium") final, who were unable to shake off a bad run of form (worse than any of the three relegated sides) that saw them lose out in the race for automatic promotion.

[Crystal Palace](/wiki/Crystal_Palace_F.C. "Crystal Palace F.C.") fared the best out of the teams relegated from the Premier League the previous season, by getting to the play-offs but losing in the semi-finals. [Norwich City](/wiki/Norwich_City_F.C. "Norwich City F.C.") never managed better than mid-table, while [Southampton](/wiki/Southampton_F.C. "Southampton F.C.") endured an awful season that saw [Sir Clive Woodward](/wiki/Sir_Clive_Woodward "Sir Clive Woodward") take up a much-criticized role as [director of football](/wiki/Director_of_football "Director of football"), manager [Harry Redknapp](/wiki/Harry_Redknapp "Harry Redknapp") return to local rivals Portsmouth and the side looking in danger of relegation for much of the season, only managing a mid-table finish with a late surge in form, thanks to the appointment of [George Burley](/wiki/George_Burley "George Burley"). Chairman [Rupert Lowe](/wiki/Rupert_Lowe "Rupert Lowe") ultimately paid the price by being forced to resign after the end of the season.

The relegation battle was principally fought by four sides: [Crewe](/wiki/Crewe_Alexandra_F.C. "Crewe Alexandra F.C."), [Brighton](/wiki/Brighton_%26_Hove_Albion_F.C. "Brighton & Hove Albion F.C."), [Millwall](/wiki/Millwall_F.C. "Millwall F.C.") and [Sheffield Wednesday](/wiki/Sheffield_Wednesday_F.C. "Sheffield Wednesday F.C."). Wednesday ultimately won the battle, and the remaining three were relegated all on the same day, after Wednesday beat Brighton 2–0. Although Crewe and Brighton had not spent long in the division and were considered to be punching above their weight, Millwall underwent a disastrous season, getting through five managers and four chairmen before relegation.

#### Football League One

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2005%E2%80%9306_in_English_football&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Football League One")]

Main article: [2005–06 Football League § League One](/wiki/2005%E2%80%9306_Football_League#League_One "2005–06 Football League")

[Southend United](/wiki/Southend_United_F.C. "Southend United F.C.") surprised many by winning a second successive promotion, returning to the Championship after nearly a decade (when it was called Division One). [Colchester United](/wiki/Colchester_United_F.C. "Colchester United F.C.") also made the Championship for the first time in their history, but their promotion was tempered by the loss of manager [Phil Parkinson](/wiki/Phil_Parkinson "Phil Parkinson") to Hull City. A highly competitive play-off race saw [Barnsley](/wiki/Barnsley_F.C. "Barnsley F.C.") emerge as winners, beating [Swansea City](/wiki/Swansea_City_A.F.C. "Swansea City A.F.C.") at the [Millennium Stadium](/wiki/Millennium_Stadium "Millennium Stadium") 4–3 on penalties after both normal time and extra-time finished 2–2, to return to the Championship after three seasons of struggle in Division Two/League One.

Following relegation and becoming the first former European champions to be subsequently relegated to the third tier of their domestic league, [Nottingham Forest](/wiki/Nottingham_Forest_F.C. "Nottingham Forest F.C.") struggled for most of the season and were in danger of suffering a second successive relegation. The departure of [Gary Megson](/wiki/Gary_Megson "Gary Megson") saw a late upturn in form and surge towards the play-offs; however, they missed out on the last day of the season, finishing 7th.

At the bottom, [Walsall](/wiki/Walsall_F.C. "Walsall F.C.") endured their second relegation in three seasons, [Swindon](/wiki/Swindon_Town_F.C. "Swindon Town F.C.") became the first former Premier League side to slip to the bottom division. [Hartlepool](/wiki/Hartlepool_United_F.C. "Hartlepool United F.C.") crashed out of the division after nearly earning promotion last season, while [MK Dons](/wiki/Milton_Keynes_Dons_F.C. "Milton Keynes Dons F.C.") suffered the relegation they only avoided the previous season when [Wrexham](/wiki/Wrexham_F.C. "Wrexham F.C.") were docked points for entering administration.

#### Football League Two

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2005%E2%80%9306_in_English_football&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Football League Two")]

Main article: [2005–06 Football League § League Two](/wiki/2005%E2%80%9306_Football_League#League_Two "2005–06 Football League")

[Carlisle United](/wiki/Carlisle_United_F.C. "Carlisle United F.C.") were another side who earned a second successive promotion, only two years after a relegation from the League that some predicted would see the end of the club. [Northampton Town](/wiki/Northampton_Town_F.C. "Northampton Town F.C.") joined them, making up for two seasons of play-off disappointment, and [Leyton Orient](/wiki/Leyton_Orient_F.C. "Leyton Orient F.C.") ended a decade in the bottom division by earning promotion in almost the last minute of the season. [Grimsby Town](/wiki/Grimsby_Town_F.C. "Grimsby Town F.C.") conceded on 91 minutes, and 14 seconds after the fans of Orient celebrated, [Lee Steele](/wiki/Lee_Steele "Lee Steele") scored to seal promotion for Orient. [Wycombe](/wiki/Wycombe_Wanderers_F.C. "Wycombe Wanderers F.C.") started the season with a 21-game unbeaten run that saw five of their players named in the PFA League Two team of the year. Two tragic off-the-field events, however, saw them fall away in the second part of the season before losing to [Cheltenham Town](/wiki/Cheltenham_Town_F.C. "Cheltenham Town F.C.") in the playoff semi-finals. Grimsby Town lost 1–0 to Cheltenham in the final at the [Millennium Stadium](/wiki/Millennium_Stadium "Millennium Stadium").

[Rushden & Diamonds](/wiki/Rushden_%26_Diamonds_F.C. "Rushden & Diamonds F.C.") failed to improve on the previous season, and paid the price with relegation to the Conference. [Oxford United](/wiki/Oxford_United_F.C. "Oxford United F.C.") joined them, despite the return of manager [Jim Smith](/wiki/Jim_Smith_(footballer,_born_1940) "Jim Smith (footballer, born 1940)"), and became the first former winners of a major trophy to be relegated to the Conference, doing so on the last day of the season.

## Retirements

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2005%E2%80%9306_in_English_football&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Retirements")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [[icon]](/wiki/File:Wiki_letter_w_cropped.svg) | This section **needs expansion**. You can help by [adding missing information](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=2005%E2%80%9306_in_English_football&action=edit&section=). *(December 2009)* |

* 22 April 2006 — [Alan Shearer](/wiki/Alan_Shearer "Alan Shearer"), 35, retires after being ruled out for the rest of the season by an injury suffered on 17 April in his 404th appearance for [Newcastle United](/wiki/Newcastle_United_F.C. "Newcastle United F.C.") since joining them in 1996. He was due to retire at the end of the season but the injury meant that the game against [Sunderland](/wiki/Sunderland_A.F.C. "Sunderland A.F.C.") was his last.[[23]](#cite_note-26)
* 8 May 2006 — [Duncan Ferguson](/wiki/Duncan_Ferguson "Duncan Ferguson"), 34, leaves [Everton](/wiki/Everton_F.C. "Everton F.C.") on a free transfer at the end of his playing career which has also taken in spells at [Dundee United](/wiki/Dundee_United_F.C. "Dundee United F.C."), [Glasgow Rangers](/wiki/Rangers_F.C. "Rangers F.C.") and Newcastle United, the last of which came between his two spells with Everton.[[24]](#cite_note-27)
* 17 May 2006 — [Dennis Bergkamp](/wiki/Dennis_Bergkamp "Dennis Bergkamp"), 37, decided to retire after the [2006 UEFA Champions League Final](/wiki/2006_UEFA_Champions_League_Final "2006 UEFA Champions League Final") defeat to [Barcelona](/wiki/FC_Barcelona "FC Barcelona"), ending an 11-year stint at [Arsenal](/wiki/Arsenal_F.C. "Arsenal F.C."). He also had experience playing for [Ajax](/wiki/AFC_Ajax "AFC Ajax") and [Internazionale](/wiki/F.C._Internazionale_Milano "F.C. Internazionale Milano"). He was ranked 2nd on the list of 50 greatest players to play for Arsenal, behind record Arsenal goalscorer, [Thierry Henry](/wiki/Thierry_Henry "Thierry Henry").
* 8 June 2006 — [Nigel Martyn](/wiki/Nigel_Martyn "Nigel Martyn"), 39, retires due to an ankle injury. He spent the final three seasons of his career at Everton having signed from [Leeds United](/wiki/Leeds_United_A.F.C. "Leeds United A.F.C.") in September 2003, and earlier in his career had spells with [Bristol Rovers](/wiki/Bristol_Rovers "Bristol Rovers") and [Crystal Palace](/wiki/Crystal_Palace_F.C. "Crystal Palace F.C."), as well as being an [England](/wiki/England_national_football_team "England national football team") goalkeeper.[[25]](#cite_note-28)
* 12 June 2006 — [Roy Keane](/wiki/Roy_Keane "Roy Keane"), 34, who until 18 November spent more than 12 years with Manchester United before joining [Celtic](/wiki/Celtic_F.C. "Celtic F.C."), retires due to a long-standing hip injury. He arrived onto the English football scene in 1990 with [Nottingham Forest](/wiki/Nottingham_Forest_F.C. "Nottingham Forest F.C.").[[26]](#cite_note-29)

## Deaths

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2005%E2%80%9306_in_English_football&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Deaths")]

* 8 September 2005 — [Noel Cantwell](/wiki/Noel_Cantwell "Noel Cantwell"), 72, died of cancer. He was a Republic of Ireland international at left-back during the 1950s and 1960s. He also captained [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United_F.C. "Manchester United F.C.")'s [FA Cup](/wiki/FA_Cup "FA Cup") winning team in 1963. As a manager he led [Coventry City](/wiki/Coventry_City_F.C. "Coventry City F.C.") to their only European campaign in 1969–70.
* 11 October 2005 - [Oscar Hold](/wiki/Oscar_Hold "Oscar Hold"), 85, played 104 league games for [Aldershot](/wiki/Aldershot_F.C. "Aldershot F.C."), [Norwich City](/wiki/Norwich_City_F.C. "Norwich City F.C."), [Notts County](/wiki/Notts_County_F.C. "Notts County F.C."), [Everton](/wiki/Everton_F.C. "Everton F.C.") and [QPR](/wiki/Queens_Park_Rangers_F.C. "Queens Park Rangers F.C.") as a forward before moving into management, where he took charge of [Doncaster Rovers](/wiki/Doncaster_Rovers_F.C. "Doncaster Rovers F.C."), [Fenerbahce](/wiki/Fenerbahce "Fenerbahce") and [Goztepe](/wiki/Goztepe_S.K. "Goztepe S.K.") of [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey"), and [Apollon Limassol](/wiki/Apollon_Limassol "Apollon Limassol") of [Cyprus](/wiki/Cyprus "Cyprus") before retiring from the game in 1984. By the time of his death, he was living in [Sunderland](/wiki/Sunderland,_Tyne_and_Wear "Sunderland, Tyne and Wear").
* 18 October 2005 — [Johnny Haynes](/wiki/Johnny_Haynes "Johnny Haynes"), 71, former [England](/wiki/England_national_football_team "England national football team") and [Fulham](/wiki/Fulham_F.C. "Fulham F.C.") midfielder, who became the first English footballer to be paid £100-a-week, died after suffering a [brain haemorrhage](/wiki/Brain_haemorrhage "Brain haemorrhage") while driving in Edinburgh, resulting in a head-on collision with another vehicle.
* 26 October 2005 — [George Swindin](/wiki/George_Swindin "George Swindin"), 90, was a former goalkeeper of [Arsenal](/wiki/Arsenal_F.C. "Arsenal F.C.") in the 1930s to the 1950s. He helped them win two league titles and one FA Cup. After retiring, he served Arsenal as manager.
* 25 November 2005 — [George Best](/wiki/George_Best "George Best"), 59, Northern-Irish-born striker who enjoyed the early and most successful years of his career Manchester United, died in [London](/wiki/London "London")'s [Cromwell Hospital](/wiki/Cromwell_Hospital "Cromwell Hospital") from [multiple organ failure](/wiki/Multiple_organ_failure "Multiple organ failure"), the result of more than 30 years of heavy drinking, three years after he underwent a liver transplant.[[27]](#cite_note-30) Personal problems meant that he played his last game for United at the age of 27, but he continued at various smaller clubs – including a brief spell with the Los Angeles Aztecs in America – until the age of 37, when he played his last professional game at [AFC Bournemouth](/wiki/AFC_Bournemouth "AFC Bournemouth"). His death dominated news bulletins and his funeral was shown live on the [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC").
* 26 December 2005 — [Ted Ditchburn](/wiki/Ted_Ditchburn "Ted Ditchburn"), 84, former England and [Tottenham](/wiki/Tottenham_Hotspur_F.C. "Tottenham Hotspur F.C.") goalkeeper.
* 14 January 2006 — [Mark Philo](/wiki/Mark_Philo "Mark Philo"), 21, [Wycombe](/wiki/Wycombe_Wanderers_F.C. "Wycombe Wanderers F.C.") winger, was killed in a car crash.
* 9 February 2006 — [Ron Greenwood](/wiki/Ron_Greenwood "Ron Greenwood"), 84, former [West Ham United](/wiki/West_Ham_United_F.C. "West Ham United F.C.") and England manager. He managed West Ham to victory in the FA Cup in 1964 and Cup Winners' Cup success a year later. Greenwood later managed the England team, achieving qualification for Euro 80 and the 1982 World Cup.
* 1 March 2006 — [Peter Osgood](/wiki/Peter_Osgood "Peter Osgood"), 59, former England striker, died of a heart attack while attending a family funeral. He played in Chelsea's 1970 FA Cup and 1971 Cup Winners' Cup victories, and won another FA Cup medal in 1976 with his next club Southampton.
* 3 April 2006 - [Billy Kiernan](/wiki/Billy_Kiernan "Billy Kiernan"), 80, played 378 league games as a left winger for [Charlton Athletic](/wiki/Charlton_Athletic_F.C. "Charlton Athletic F.C.") between 1949 and 1961 after playing in [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong") where he was stationed with the [Royal Ulster Rifles](/wiki/Royal_Ulster_Rifles "Royal Ulster Rifles"). He also starred for the [London XI](/wiki/London_XI "London XI") which participated in the [Inter-Cities Fairs Cup](/wiki/Inter-Cities_Fairs_Cup "Inter-Cities Fairs Cup") in the late 1950s.
* 18 April 2006 — [John Lyall](/wiki/John_Lyall "John Lyall"), 66, former West Ham United and [Ipswich Town](/wiki/Ipswich_Town_F.C. "Ipswich Town F.C.") manager, died of a heart attack. He completed his first season in management at West Ham with FA Cup glory in 1975, winning the trophy again in 1980 and taking West Ham to their highest-ever league position – third – in 1986. He was sacked when West Ham were relegated in 1989, but made a comeback the following year with Ipswich Town. Lyall took Ipswich into the inaugural Premier League as Second Division champions in 1992, and remained in charge for another 2+1⁄2 years before he was sacked in December 1994. Lyall never returned to management after his dismissal from Ipswich.
* 24 April 2006 — [Brian Labone](/wiki/Brian_Labone "Brian Labone"), 66, former [Everton](/wiki/Everton_F.C. "Everton F.C.") captain who played in Harry Catterick's successful 1960s side, died of a heart attack. He helped them win the FA Cup in 1966 as well as the league title in 1963 and 1970. Labone was also capped 26 times by England between 1962 and 1970, but did not make the squad for England's victorious 1966 World Cup campaign.
